title: The problem with binary significance
text:
In damages cases, econometric evidence is often discussed in terms of whether an estimated effect is statistically significant. This can be useful, but it can also mislead.
A statistically significant estimate is evidence against a specified null hypothesis, often a zero-effect hypothesis. But a statistically insignificant estimate does not prove that the effect is zero.

A statistically insignificant estimate means that the observed estimate is not sufficiently incompatible with the null hypothesis under the chosen testing rule. It does not mean that there was no effect.

title: The problem with binary significance
text:
In damages cases, econometric evidence is often discussed in terms of whether an estimated effect is statistically significant. This can be useful, but it can also mislead.
A statistically significant estimate is evidence against a specified null hypothesis, often a zero-effect hypothesis. But a statistically insignificant estimate does not prove that the effect is zero.

A statistically insignificant estimate means that the observed estimate is not sufficiently incompatible with the null hypothesis under the chosen testing rule. It does not mean that there was no effect.

title: The problem with binary significance
The problem with binary significance
text:
Visual intuition

Static figure to add here: distribution of the estimate, null hypothesis, confidence interval, and significance threshold.

title: The problem with binary significance
text:
The key limitation
The standard significance test asks a narrow question:

If the null hypothesis were true, would an estimate this far from the null be unusual?

That is not the same as asking:

Which economically meaningful overcharges remain plausible after seeing this estimate?

This second question is the starting point for severity analysis.

title: Beyond Statistical Significance
text:
Statistical significance is often treated as a bright line: significant estimates are taken seriously, insignificant estimates are sometimes treated as if they showed no effect.
This companion website explains why that interpretation is too crude, especially in follow-on damages cases. It introduces severity analysis as a way to ask a more informative post-data question: which effect sizes remain plausible in light of the estimate actually observed?

Start with the problem Download the paper

title: Beyond Statistical Significance
text:
What this companion website does
This website does not duplicate the paper. It provides a visual and interactive explanation of the main ideas.


1. The problem
Why “not statistically significant” does not mean “no effect”, “no harm”, or “zero overcharge”.
Go to the problem


2. Severity analysis
How severity analysis asks which hypothetical effect sizes would probably have produced stronger evidence if they were true.
Go to severity


3. Explorer
A future interactive calculator where users can vary the estimate, standard error, degrees of freedom, and hypothetical claim.
Go to explorer


4. Legal implications
What courts and experts should, and should not, infer from statistically insignificant estimates.
Go to implications

title: Beyond Statistical Significance
text:
How to read this website
Start with the problem page if you want the intuition. Then move to the severity page for the alternative framework. The explorer page will later allow readers to experiment with different assumptions.

title: Severity explorer
text:
Technical note
Because this website is hosted on GitHub Pages, the interactive version should be implemented as a static client-side tool, for example using JavaScript or Observable JS inside Quarto. A Shiny app would require a separate server.

title: Paper and downloads

Version
This is the first public version of the companion website. Future versions will add visual storytelling and an interactive severity calculator.

title: Implications for damages cases
text:
A statistically insignificant estimate should not automatically be read as evidence of no harm. In particular, courts should avoid treating insignificance as if it established that the true effect was zero.

Insignificance is not proof of absence. It is a statement about the strength of the evidence against a specific null hypothesis under a specific testing procedure.

title: Implications for damages cases
text:
A statistically insignificant estimate should not automatically be read as evidence of no harm. In particular, courts should avoid treating insignificance as if it established that the true effect was zero.

Insignificance is not proof of absence. It is a statement about the strength of the evidence against a specific null hypothesis under a specific testing procedure.

title: Implications for damages cases
text:
What courts can ask instead
A more informative evidentiary question is:

Which overcharge magnitudes are still plausible in light of the estimate and its uncertainty?

This reframes the role of econometric evidence. The estimate is not merely classified as significant or insignificant. It is used to assess the range of effect sizes that remain compatible with the evidence.

title: Implications for damages cases
section: Why this matters in follow-on damages cases
text:
Why this matters in follow-on damages cases
In follow-on damages cases, courts often need to assess whether an infringement caused harm and, if so, the likely magnitude of that harm.
A binary significance threshold does not answer that question. Severity analysis helps connect the statistical evidence to the substantive damages question by focusing on economically relevant effect sizes.

title: Implications for damages cases
What severity asks instead
text:
Severity analysis changes the focus. Instead of stopping at “significant” or “not significant”, it asks what the observed result tells us about particular hypothetical effect sizes.
A useful way to express the severity question is:

Given what we observed, which hypothetical effect sizes would probably have produced a stronger result if they were true?

title: What severity asks instead
text:
Severity analysis changes the focus. Instead of stopping at “significant” or “not significant”, it asks what the observed result tells us about particular hypothetical effect sizes.
A useful way to express the severity question is:

Given what we observed, which hypothetical effect sizes would probably have produced a stronger result if they were true?

title: What severity asks instead
text:
Why this helps
Suppose an estimated overcharge is statistically insignificant. That alone does not prove that the true overcharge is zero. But the estimate may still be informative.
If the estimate is precise, then large hypothetical overcharges may be difficult to reconcile with the observed result. If the estimate is imprecise, then even sizeable overcharges may remain plausible.
Severity analysis therefore links the interpretation of the estimate to the uncertainty around it.

title: What severity asks instead
What severity asks instead
text:
Visual intuition

Static figure to add here: observed estimate, uncertainty distribution, hypothetical overcharge claim, and severity area.

title: What severity asks instead
text:
Core interpretation
If a hypothetical overcharge would probably have produced a stronger result than the one actually observed, then the failure to observe such a stronger result counts against that hypothetical overcharge.
If, however, the observed estimate would not be unusual even if the hypothetical overcharge were true, then the data do not strongly rule it out.
